content:
6 min read
Just now

--

We have all been there. You run your local integration tests, and the green checkmarks appear in milliseconds. You hit your endpoint with a quick curl command, and the JSON response snaps back instantly. You feel like a genius. You tell your team that the new service is ready for production, rock-solid, and blazingly fast.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Your API is not fast until the metrics prove it.I was that developer last Tuesday. Then, I actually measured it under load.
The Illusion of Localhost
My team was preparing to launch a new notification microservice. It was a standard Spring Boot application using Java 21 and Spring Web. The logic was straightforward: accept a payload, validate the user permissions, save the transaction to a PostgreSQL database, and push a message to an external third-party API.
On my local machine, everything felt perfect. But local testing is a dangerous lie. It lacks network latency, concurrent users, and realistic database contention.
To prepare for production, I decided to run a simple load test using Locust. I targeted a modest five hundred concurrent users. What happened next was a brutal reality check.
The Baseline Disaster
My beautiful, snappy API did not just slow down. It choked.
